data.title: Utz signals EBITDA margin expansion and revised EPS guidance amid infrastructure investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Utz Brands, Inc. (UTZ) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Howard A. Friedman reported confidence in the company's full-year EBITDA outlook, stating that investments made to drive top-line growth are “coming through” and highlighted that “Q3 is typically a significant step up versus Q2.” Friedman emphasized accelerated capital expenditure (CapEx) supporting productivity savings, a recently announced plant closure impacting the back half of the year, and a positive portfolio mix. He added, “we feel like it's -- we're in a good place on the guide and have some clarity and line of sight to the productivity savings and the margin expansion as we progress through the rest of the year.”
  • Friedman indicated that distribution gains, improved C-store performance, and investment in geographic expansion are supporting strong top-line results, as “measured and unmeasured channels, all kind of moving together very positively.”
  • On the revision to EPS guidance, CFO William J. Kelley said, “The original guide that we put in place was the EPS growth of 10% to 15%. We revised that in our print yesterday to be -- or this morning to be 7% to 10%.” Kelley attributed this to higher CapEx spending, increased cash interest, and higher depreciation and amortization, explaining, “On the interest side...we borrowed a bit more sooner...that's picking up there in terms of more interest. On the CapEx side, we did confirm a higher spend on the -- higher end of our range on CapEx and we spent more of that more quickly than in the past.” Kelley also stated, “we are confident that 2025 will be the peak of our CapEx spending.”

Outlook

  • Management maintained confidence in achieving full-year EBITDA growth, with productivity savings and margin expansion expected to accelerate in the second half of the year. Friedman said, “we obviously nudged up the low end from 6 to 10 to 7 to 10.”
  • EPS growth guidance was revised to 7% to 10%, down from the previous 10% to 15%, due to increased interest and depreciation from accelerated CapEx. Kelley noted, “the impact of our revision, the midpoint to midpoint is about $0.03. Half of that is interest. The other half is D&A.”
  • Management stated that 2025 will be the peak for CapEx spending.

Financial Results

  • Management referenced strong top-line momentum in the first half, with distribution gains in both core and expansion geographies, supported by increased infrastructure and marketing investments. Friedman stated, “distribution gains supporting it. And then we've been making investments to drive that westward expansion, and they're also coming through the top line results.”
  • EBITDA was reported as “roughly flat in the first half of the year,” but management expects “high-teens growth in the back half.”
  • The company reported that CapEx is “about 70% spent through the first half of the year.”

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: Asked about the company’s confidence in achieving 8.5% EBITDA growth for the year after a muted first half. Friedman cited CapEx-driven productivity, plant closures, and favorable portfolio mix as drivers for back-half improvement.
  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Sought clarity on EPS guidance revision. Kelley detailed the shift, attributing it to “higher CapEx spend...and more amortization and depreciation.”
  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: Inquired about distribution gains, particularly in the Midwest. Friedman explained that gains are broad-based, with “very strong retailer support from national chains” and momentum in club, discount, and dollar channels.
  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: Asked if elevated SG&A investment will continue. Friedman confirmed second-half SG&A will be “modestly higher” as infrastructure and marketing spend persists, supporting top-line momentum.
  • Brian Holland, D.A. Davidson: Queried about promotional intensity. Friedman noted the category is “fairly stable” and that the company’s guide assumes no significant improvement in the category.
  • John Baumgartner, Mizuho: Asked about DSD investments and C-store performance. Friedman described ongoing infrastructure investment and route optimization, with C-store momentum improving and expected to be “flattish by the end of the year.”
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Asked about supply chain and marketing strategy. Friedman and Kelley highlighted progress in manufacturing optimization, automation, and increased marketing spend, stating, “our automation is now in place and is now active.”
  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Asked about top-line drivers and value proposition. Friedman cited expanded distribution, in-store support, and infrastructure investment as key drivers, with the value proposition remaining unchanged despite the conclusion of bonus packs.
  • Peter Grom, UBS: Inquired about long-term category growth and protein innovation. Friedman expressed bullishness on the category and acknowledged ongoing interest in protein-based snack innovation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about the EPS guidance revision and sought reassurance on back-half EBITDA acceleration and strategic investments, with several referencing the stock’s negative reaction during the call. The tone was slightly negative, particularly around the EPS downgrade and CapEx spend.
  • Management maintained a confident tone on productivity, margin expansion, and strategic execution, though more defensive and explanatory when discussing EPS guidance changes. Friedman repeatedly emphasized clarity and confidence in EBITDA growth and productivity savings. The sentiment was positive on business fundamentals, slightly defensive when addressing financial guidance revisions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed more urgency and scrutiny regarding guidance and capital deployment, with management presenting more detailed justifications for strategic investments and EPS guidance changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter included a downward revision to EPS guidance (now 7%-10% growth), contrasted with the prior quarter’s 10%-15% range. EBITDA outlook was reaffirmed with a slight increase in the lower bound, from 6%-10% to 7%-10%.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward infrastructure and CapEx acceleration, with management emphasizing near-term margin expansion and long-term productivity. Previous quarter’s focus was on distribution gains and bonus pack program; current quarter highlights infrastructure, plant closures, and automation.
  • Analysts shifted from questions about market expansion and product performance to more pointed inquiries about financial mechanics, guidance revisions, and capital allocation.
  • Both quarters emphasized Boulder Canyon’s performance and geographic expansion, but current management tone was more cautious and detailed in financial explanations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited increased interest expense and accelerated depreciation as factors impacting EPS.
  • CapEx spending is at a historical peak, with 70% deployed in the first half, raising questions about balance sheet impact and return on investment.
  • Analysts raised concerns over promotional intensity, category stagnation, and the sustainability of top-line momentum as the category remains “fairly stable.”
  • Management acknowledged ongoing risk from supply chain optimization and plant closures but expressed confidence these actions would drive margin expansion.

Final Takeaway

Utz Brands management emphasized continued progress in productivity and geographic expansion, while acknowledging higher CapEx and related impacts on EPS guidance. The company remains confident in delivering EBITDA margin expansion through the second half of the year, supported by infrastructure investments, automation, and a strong performance from brands such as Boulder Canyon. While short-term earnings guidance has been revised downward due to higher interest and depreciation, management maintains a positive outlook on long-term value creation and operational execution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Builders FirstSource outlines $14.8B–$15.6B sales guidance as market conditions remain challenging
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Builders FirstSource (BLDR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter M. Jackson stated the company is "prioritizing what's within our control; serving customers with excellence, leveraging technology and managing the business with discipline" to strengthen its position amid a challenging market. He emphasized continued expansion of value-added solutions and technology, referencing over $35 million invested in value-added solutions in Q2, including a new millwork facility in Florida and plant upgrades in seven states.
  • Jackson reported, "We generated $5 million in productivity savings in Q2, primarily through targeted supply chain initiatives," and highlighted an on-time and in-full delivery rate of 92%, even as eight facilities were consolidated year-to-date.
  • The company acquired Truckee-Tahoe Lumber, extending its reach in Northern California and Nevada, and deployed over $500 million toward capital priorities, including organic growth, M&A, and share repurchases in the quarter.
  • Jackson announced the appointment of Gayatri Narayan as President of Technology and Digital Solutions, citing her "over 2 decades of global technology leadership experience." He noted, "Since launch in early 2024, we have seen more than $2 billion of orders and $4 billion of quotes placed through our BFS digital tools. These metrics are up more than 400% and nearly 300% year-to-date, respectively, compared to 2024."
  • CFO Pete R. Beckmann stated, "Net sales decreased 5%, $4.2 billion, driven by lower organic sales and commodity deflation, partially offset by growth from acquisitions." Beckmann explained, "Adjusted EBITDA was $506 million, down 24%, primarily driven by lower gross profit... Adjusted EPS was $2.38, a decrease of 32% compared to the prior year."

Outlook

  • Beckmann provided updated 2025 guidance: "We are guiding net sales in the range of $14.8 billion to $15.6 billion. We expect adjusted EBITDA to be $1.5 billion to $1.7 billion. Adjusted EBITDA margin is forecast to be in the range of 10.1% to 10.9%."
  • He added, "We expect Q3 net sales to be between $3.65 and $3.95 billion given a weaker-than-normal building season. Q3 adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $375 million and $425 million."
  • The company revised its gross margin outlook: "We expect our 2025 full year gross margin to be below long-term normalized levels and in a range of 29% to 30.5%. We expect free cash flow of $800 million to $1 billion."
  • Jackson commented, "Our latest forecast assumes Single-Family is down 10% to 12% for the year. We continue to expect a multi-family headwind to sales of $400 million to $500 million and a headwind to EBITDA of less than $200 million, with most of the drag already coming in the first half of the year."

Financial Results

  • Beckmann detailed, "Gross profit was $1.3 billion, a decrease of 11% compared to the prior year period. Gross margin was 30.7%, down 210 basis points. Adjusted SG&A of $818 million increased $4 million, primarily attributable to acquired operations."
  • Operating cash flow was $341 million, a decrease of $111 million, mainly attributable to lower net income; free cash flow was $255 million. The company reported a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 2.3x and completed a $750 million senior unsecured notes offering.
  • Capital expenditures were $86 million; $61 million was deployed on acquisitions, and $391 million spent on repurchasing 3.3 million shares.

Q&A

  • Matthew Adrien Bouley, Barclays: Asked about competitive positioning and margin drivers. Jackson responded, "A big piece of that is around technology, both in the core systems, but as well as digital... Digital is another way where we have seen customers benefit in terms of utilizing both online tools, but also the 3-dimensional digital twin to optimize their build process."
  • Bouley also questioned Q2 gross margins. Beckmann said, "We were very pleased with that margin performance for Q2... that 20 basis points increase over Q1, that's worth about $8 million."
  • Michael Glaser Dahl, RBC: Probed on margin guidance and market dynamics. Jackson replied, "The competitive environment... it's pretty stable, it's a tough market. There's a fight out there in terms of what we're seeing when we're quoting and looking at what's going on with share according to our calcs. We think things are pretty well stabilized on the share side."
  • Dahl asked about truss capacity and margins. Jackson explained, "We're underutilized from a capacity perspective and trust, given where starts are... we mothball operations or close operations or consolidate in order to ensure that the remaining facilities do have enough to stay stable."
  • John Lovallo, UBS: Asked about Canadian lumber tariffs. Beckmann stated, "We have factored in the duties and increases into our guidance for the lumber side... I don't think will hit us really in our numbers for at least 3 to 4 months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was slightly negative, focusing on concerns around margin pressure, capacity utilization, and the impact of tariffs. Questions repeatedly circled around market share, profitability, and risk to guidance, with pressing requests for clarity on headwinds and cost controls.
  • Management maintained a neutral-to-slightly positive tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing adaptability and operational discipline. However, during Q&A, responses occasionally shifted toward a more cautious or defensive posture, as seen in phrases such as "we're very confident that we are as efficient or better than anybody else" and acknowledgment that "it's a competitive environment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited increased concern about the pace of market recovery and margin sustainability, though management continued to stress readiness for eventual market improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 net sales was revised downward from $16.05–$17.05 billion last quarter to $14.8–$15.6 billion, reflecting weaker expectations for single-family starts and commodity prices.
  • The EBITDA guidance range narrowed and moved lower, and full-year gross margin expectations were trimmed below long-term normalized levels.
  • The strategic emphasis on digital adoption and operational efficiency was reiterated, but management noted a slower-than-expected pace of digital tool adoption.
  • Analysts this quarter focused even more on cost discipline, downside scenarios, and mitigation plans for persistent volume softness.
  • Management's tone shifted from resilient optimism last quarter to a more measured and risk-aware approach, frequently referencing the challenging market environment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent softness in single-family and multi-family starts, ongoing affordability issues, and the risk of further commodity price declines—especially for OSB—as challenges.
  • The company is consolidating facilities and managing headcount to align capacity with demand, and closely monitoring discretionary spend.
  • Analysts expressed concern over capacity utilization, the impact of tariffs, and the sustainability of margins in a low-volume environment.
  • The pace of M&A was described as slow due to market volatility and price discovery challenges, though management expects inorganic investments to be important longer-term.

Final Takeaway

Builders FirstSource leadership emphasized its disciplined strategy and investment in digital tools and operational efficiency to navigate a slower housing market. Updated guidance reflects ongoing headwinds in single-family and multi-family demand, with cost controls and capital allocation remaining central priorities. Management highlighted that its operational improvements, technology investments, and prudent capital deployment position the company to accelerate growth as market conditions improve, while remaining committed to maximizing shareholder value through the current downcycle.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Psychedelic stocks up after report on AbbVie-Gilgamesh deal talks
data.description:
AbbVie’s (NYSE:ABBV) move to buy New York-based psychedelic drug developer Gilgamesh Pharmaceuticals in a deal worth around $1B, according to Bloomberg News, rallied the psychedelic space on Thursday.

Citing people familiar with the matter, Bloomberg News added that Peter Thiel-backed Atai Life Sciences (NASDAQ:ATAI) has engaged an investment bank to assess early-stage interest from leading industry players.

In addition to Atai (NASDAQ:ATAI), Cybin (NYSE:CYBN), and Mind Medicine (MND),  also traded higher in reaction. COMPASS Pathways (NASDAQ:CMPS) added ~13% following its better-than-expected second-quarter earnings for 2025.

Privately held Gilgamesh, which focuses on developing drugs for mental conditions including depression and anxiety, touted positive results from a mid-stage trial for its lead asset GM-2505 against major depressive disorder in May.

Gilgamesh’s therapeutic focus, neuroplastogens are considered next-generation psychedelic treatments characterized by a rapid onset of action with a lower incidence of hallucinations.

A potential M&A deal between AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) and Gilgamesh could “increase the Street’s confidence that psychedelics are an investable space,” Bloomberg News reported, quoting Jefferies analysts, who also cited a favorable regulatory backdrop for the drug class.

The analysts noted that Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and FDA Commissioner Marty Makary have recently voiced support for psychedelics.

data.title: Obsidian Energy launches offer to buy $48.4M of outstanding notes
data.description:
• Obsidian Energy (NYSE:OBE) (TSX:OBE:CA) has launched a cash offer to purchase up to $48.4 million of its outstanding 11.95% Senior Unsecured Notes due July 27, 2027.
  • As of July 31, 2025, a total of $112.2 million in notes remained outstanding. The offer is set to expire at 5:00 p.m. EDT on August 12, 2025, unless extended.

data.title: Oxford Square Capital proposes notes offering
data.description:
• Oxford Square Capital (NASDAQ:OXSQ) has launched a public offering of notes, with pricing and terms to be finalized through negotiations with underwriters.
  • A 30-day option may also be granted to purchase additional notes to cover any over-allotments.
  • The notes are expected to be listed on the NASDAQ Global Select Market and begin trading within 30 days of issuance.
  • Proceeds from the offering will be used to repay debt, invest according to the company’s strategy, and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: MYR Group signals strong T&D growth with new $500M MSA and 4% backlog increase amid expanding electrification demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MYR Group Inc. (MYRG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Richard S. Swartz highlighted "a steady second quarter performance resulted from the strength of our long-term customer relationships, operational consistency and strong market presence." Swartz noted expansion through several master service agreements (MSAs), furthering key customer relationships and capturing projects in core markets. Emphasis remains on grid modernization, electrification, and technology-driven demand, particularly artificial intelligence, as ongoing market drivers.

  • Swartz stated that "the increased electrification and investments being made in the electrical infrastructure are encouraging and highlight why we believe our chosen markets are poised for ongoing success for years to come."

  • CFO Kelly Michelle Huntington reported, "Our second quarter 2025 revenues were $900 million, which represents an increase of $71 million or 8.6% compared to the same period last year." She also highlighted a gross margin of 11.5% compared to 4.9% last year. Net income for the quarter was $27 million, a reversal from a net loss of $15 million in the prior year period, and net income per diluted share was $1.70 compared to negative $0.91.

  • Brian K. Stern, COO of Transmission & Distribution, announced the execution of a 5-year design, build electric distribution MSA with Xcel Energy, with anticipated revenues "to be in excess of $500 million over the 5-year period" and additional MSAs with major utilities in the Northeast and Midwest. Stern emphasized strong project wins and continued robust bidding activity.

  • Don A. Egan, COO of Commercial & Industrial, confirmed the contractual award and backlog addition of Phase 1 of a large-scale data center project in Colorado valued at over $90 million. Egan also cited awards in aerospace, healthcare, higher education, battery storage, transportation, and manufacturing.

Outlook

  • Management expressed ongoing confidence in the demand for electricity and infrastructure investment. Swartz reiterated, "our focus remains on collaborating closely with our customers in an open and trusting partnership while delivering safe, quality and consistent on-time results in this dynamic energy landscape."

  • Huntington reaffirmed expectations for "high single-digit growth, excluding solar," for both C&I and T&D segments for the full year, noting continued market strength and unpredictability in quarterly revenue timing.

  • No explicit changes in guidance language or targets compared to the prior quarter.

Financial Results

  • Revenues for Q2 2025 were $900 million. T&D revenues reached $506 million, with $305 million for Transmission and $201 million for Distribution. Distribution revenues increased by $25 million and Transmission by $23 million versus the same period last year.

  • Gross margin improved to 11.5% from 4.9% a year earlier, primarily due to better-than-anticipated productivity and a favorable job closeout, partially offset by increased labor and project inefficiencies as well as unfavorable change orders.

  • T&D operating income margin was 8% (up from an operating loss margin of 1.8% in the prior year period), and C&I operating income margin was 5.6% (up from 0.4%).

  • Net income was $27 million, net income per diluted share was $1.70, and EBITDA was $56 million (compared to negative $5 million last year).

  • Operating cash flow was $33 million, with free cash flow of $12 million. Total backlog as of June 30, 2025, was $2.64 billion, up 4% year over year. Working capital stood at $251 million, funded debt at $86 million, and borrowing availability at $383 million.

  • The Board authorized a new $75 million share repurchase program replacing the prior program.

Q&A

  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc: Asked about the Xcel Energy MSA, whether it was new scope or a renewal, and if there was any displacement. Swartz responded, "It's new scope. So it's additional to what we already have under our MSAs... This is additional work."

  • Jain followed up on the sequential decline in C&I backlog. Swartz explained, "Our backlog is always going to be lumpy. I've said that for years... These projects are longer-term projects in a lot of cases... can take months to negotiate out."

  • Atidrip Modak, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about business footprint expansion and further MSA opportunities. Swartz said, "We're always -- I mean, we like the MSA work... we like the bid work too. So it's really just timing how it comes in... But this last quarter, we did have some pretty good MSA activity."

  • Modak also asked about labor sourcing and margin impact from new MSAs. Swartz replied, "For the most part, we've always self-performed 100% of our electrical work... always looking for that right, tuck-in acquisition... patient and make the right decisions."

  • Justin Hauke, Baird: Asked for an update on solar revenue contribution. Huntington said, "If you look on the T&D side of our revenues, it was 10% of our revenues last year... down to just 4% of our fourth quarter revenues and that percentage has continued to decline in the first and second quarters."

  • Hauke also questioned capital allocation between M&A and buybacks. Swartz stated, "It's finding the right acquisition... we're going to be disciplined in the price we'll pay... with our balance sheet, with the shape it's in... we've got the opportunities to still do acquisitions, push our organic growth and do stock buybacks when it makes sense."

  • Jonathan Paul Braatz, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about the need to accelerate CapEx and corporate expenses in response to demand. Swartz replied, "We watch that all the time, especially on the CapEx side... continue to make the right calls, buy the equipment and advance, make the right capital expenditures as needed."

  • Braatz also asked about project delays due to tariffs/supply chain. Egan responded, "Haven't necessarily seen many projects extend their schedules. However, we do have clients that are coming to us much sooner... to get long lead equipment coming."

  • Brian Brophy, Stifel: Inquired about growth outlook for T&D. Huntington answered, "We still see that expectation for the full year, that high single-digit growth, excluding solar... but continue to see a strong market outlook."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally positive, with questions focusing on growth opportunities, backlog dynamics, capital allocation, and market-specific risks. Analysts probed for clarity on the visibility of project awards, the sustainability of margins, and capital deployment strategies.

  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing operational strength, healthy market demand, and balanced capital allocation. Swartz used phrases such as "we continue to be proactive and disciplined in this dynamic energy landscape" and Huntington reaffirmed strong growth expectations.

  • Compared to the prior quarter, both analysts and management showed increased confidence, with more emphasis on new project wins, strong backlog, and improved margins, while remaining cautious on the impacts of market volatility and selectivity in project bidding.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marked an increase in revenue, margin, net income, and backlog compared to the previous quarter. Q2 revenue was $900 million, up from $834 million in Q1; gross margin was 11.5%, consistent with 11.6% in the prior quarter.

  • Notable strategic developments included the new $500 million MSA with Xcel Energy, the contractual award of the $90 million data center project, and a new $75 million share repurchase program.

  • Analysts' focus shifted slightly more toward long-term backlog stability and capital allocation flexibility, while management maintained a steady, confident tone, emphasizing the ability to self-fund growth and pursue acquisitions or buybacks as opportunities arise.

  • No major changes in guidance language; the company continues to project high single-digit growth in core segments, excluding solar, consistent with the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged increased costs related to labor, project inefficiencies, and unfavorable change orders as partial offsets to margin improvement.

  • Swartz addressed the inherent lumpiness of backlog and the need for patience in contract negotiations, especially for large projects.

  • Egan noted that while project delays due to tariffs and supply chain issues are not widespread, clients are issuing earlier notices to proceed to secure long-lead equipment.

Final Takeaway

MYR Group's Q2 2025 results reflect continued operational strength, robust demand in both T&D and C&I segments, and strategic wins such as the new $500 million MSA with Xcel Energy. Improved margin performance, solid backlog, and disciplined capital allocation—including a new share repurchase authorization—underscore management’s confidence in sustained growth opportunities driven by electrification and infrastructure investment. The company remains focused on executing projects efficiently, expanding customer relationships, and selectively pursuing new business in a dynamic market environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mortgage rates remain in narrow range for fourth straight week
data.description:
Mortgage rates remained within the same narrow range for the fourth consecutive week, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.72% as of July 31, down slightly from 6.74% last week and 6.73% in the same period of 2024.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.85%, down from 5.87% a week ago and 5.99% a year ago.

"Continued economic growth, along with moderating house prices and rising inventory, bodes well for buyers and sellers alike," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the past four weeks:

data.title: Aqua Metals to execute reverse stock split
data.description:
• Aqua Metals (NASDAQ:AQMS) plans to execute a reverse stock split of its common stock at a ratio of 1 post-split share for every 10 pre-split shares.
  • The reverse stock split will become effective at on August 4, 2025. The action will reduce the number of issued shares of the company's common stock from 13,948,763 shares to around 1,394,876 shares.
  • Shares fell nearly 10%.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump on trade deals with Mexico, Canada, South Korea
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks. 

  • President Trump announced Thursday morning that trade negotiations with Mexico have been extended for 90 days.

"I have just concluded a telephone conversation with the President of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, which was very successful in that, more and more, we are getting to know and understand each other. The complexities of a Deal with Mexico are somewhat different than other Nations because of both the problems, and assets, of the Border," Trump said in a long post on Truth Social. 

"We have agreed to extend, for a 90 Day period, the exact same Deal as we had for the last short period of time, namely, that Mexico will continue to pay a 25% Fentanyl Tariff, 25% Tariff on Cars, and 50% Tariff on Steel, Aluminum, and Copper. Additionally, Mexico has agreed to immediately terminate its Non Tariff Trade Barriers, of which there were many. We will be talking to Mexico over the next 90 Days with the goal of signing a Trade Deal somewhere within the 90 Day period of time, or longer," Trump added.

  • Meanwhile, Trump said that Canada's announcement that it plans to officially recognize the Palestinian state in September along with the U.K. and France will complicate a trade deal with the U.S.

"Wow! Canada has just announced that it is backing statehood for Palestine. That will make it very hard for us to make a Trade Deal with them. Oh,Canada!!!" Trump said in a post on Truth Social. 

  • Trump also announced a trade deal with South Korea that calls for a 15% tariff on South Korean goods, along with South Korean investments in the U.S. and purchases of energy products.

"The Deal is that South Korea will give to the United States $350 Billion Dollars for Investments owned and controlled by the United States, and selected by myself, as President. Additionally, South Korea will purchase $100 Billion Dollars of LNG, or other Energy products and, further, South Korea has agreed to invest a large sum of money for their Investment purposes. This sum will be announced within the next two weeks when the President of South Korea, Lee Jae Myung, comes to the White House for a Bilateral Meeting," Trump said in a post on Truth Social. 

"It is also agreed that South Korea will be completely OPEN TO TRADE with the United States, and that they will accept American product including Cars and Trucks, Agriculture, etc. We have agreed to a Tariff for South Korea of 15%. America will not be charged a Tariff," Trump added.

  • Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that the U.S. and China are getting closer to signing a trade deal.

“I believe that we have the makings of a deal,” Bessent said during an interview on CNBC. “There’s still a few technical details to be worked out on the Chinese side between us. I’m confident that it will be done, but it’s not 100% done.”

The U.S. and China have until Aug. 12 to agree to a deal before higher tariffs kick in for both countries.

data.title: Ford's 'Model T' moment, capital reallocation from EVs takes pressure off shares
data.description:
Ford (NYSE:F) is clawing back some recent losses as investors reassess its solid Q2 results and the strategic shift of diverting funds from its unprofitable EV unit to its cash cow Ford Pro division.

To accelerate the high-margin and reoccurring revenue of Ford Pro, CEO Jim Farley said on the earnings call, the company will “reallocate the resources on future EV programs to accelerate progress over [the company’s] multiyear business plan…and shift capital towards Pro.”

Farley also announced plans to build a “breakthrough” electric vehicle and platform in the U.S.

“This is a Model T moment for us at Ford,” Farley said of the new low-cost EV, presenting a chance to bring in “a new family of vehicles to the world that offer incredible technology, efficiency, space and features.”

These developments helped offset some of the pressure on shares from anticipated tariff headwinds, but analysts remain cautious that ongoing affordability issues and costs associated with reshoring manufacturing could make it difficult for the company to deliver consistent results through the rest of FY25, especially as warranty issues spill into H2 results. 

Additionally, establishing a profitable EV business in the current environment (expiration of EV tax credits, margin pressures) will become even more problematic after Chinese EV tech migrates into the U.S.

“While we expect the company to put a 'brave face' on their EV strategy…investors remain skeptical for how Ford could operate a profitable EV business when even Tesla’s car business is unprofitable on an underlying basis,” Morgan Stanley’s Adam Jonas says.

Tariffs are also keeping a lid on Ford’s (NYSE:F) profitability, especially after the company said it expects a $2B headwind for 2025, leading to a downgrade in EBIT guidance for the year.

(Note: As we go to press, the U.S. and Mexico just announced a 90-day pause on tariffs, keeping the 25% import tax in place. The news is giving a modest lift to U.S. automakers).  

However, in the event the two nations hammer out a deal before the end of October, Evercore ISI’s Chris McNally believes Ford (NYSE:F) can regain 15 to 30 cents of EPS “as Mexico is the majority of their tariff exposure through 400K of Mustang Mach E, Bronco, and Maverick production.”

Adds BofA Securities’ Federico Merendi, “We think Ford’s liquidity level is sufficient to weather potential challenges from production pressures, future product launches and tariffs.”

After setting a 6-month high at the open, Ford (F) shares have retreated, but are still holding onto a 2% gain from Wednesday’s close. Shares are up 9.6% year-to-date versus +2.4% and -31% for rivals General Motors (GM), and Stellantis (STLA), respectively.
